60529.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/pony-alpha
This entry refers to the fourth sutta of the Kilesasaṃyutta (SN 27.4). The compound is formed from samphassa (contact) and sutta (discourse). The sutta discusses how desire and lust regarding contact are defilements of consciousness.

70282.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/pony-alpha
This entry refers to the Vedanā Sutta (SN 47.49) within the Satipaṭṭhāna Saṃyutta. The discourse specifically concerns the contemplation of feeling (vedanā) as one of the four establishments of mindfulness.

30867.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/pony-alpha
SN 22.84. This sutta, the second of that name in the Khandhasaṃyutta, is addressed to the monk Tissa and concerns the teaching of the five aggregates (khandhas).

51662.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
This entry records the second Mahā Kappina Sutta, a neuter noun compound meaning “Great Kappina discourse”, found in SN 54.7 of the Anāpanasutta section.

35366.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Refers to SN 35.131, the 'Nakula‑pitu‑sutta', a neuter noun compound (kammadhāraya) meaning 'the discourse of Nakula's father'.

35364.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The noun nakulapitarā appears as a householder’s name, literally “Nakula’s father”, following the common pattern of personal names formed with a personal name + pitṛ.
35364.sutta_1: atha kho nakulapitā gahapati yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinno kho nakulapitā gahapati bhagavantaṃ etadavoca:
35364.example_1: Then the householder Nakula’s father went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha:

73332.meaning_1: ambrosia; drink of immortality; divine nectar
73332.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence the word appears in the instrumental case (amatena) meaning “by the death‑less ambrosia”, i.e. the immortal nectar of the Dhamma talk. The form is a neuter noun derived from the negative prefix na + the verb marati “to die” with the taddhita suffix –ta, literally “not‑dying”.
73332.sutta_1: idānāhaṃ, bhante, bhagavatā dhammiyā kathāya amatena abhisitto”ti.
73332.example_1: Just now the Buddha anointed me with the deathless ambrosia of a Dhamma talk.”

34070.meaning_1: the sutta of the gods' speech
34070.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the second sutta of the Khandha Saṃyutta, titled “Devadahasutta”. It is a neuter noun compound meaning “the sutta of the gods’ speech”. No further morphological derivation is known.

39627.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the contextual sentence the word pacchābhūmaṃ denotes a western land or region, indicating the place to which the speaker wishes to go and settle.
39627.sutta_1: “icchāma mayaṃ, bhante, pacchābhūmaṃ janapadaṃ gantuṃ, pacchābhūme janapade nivāsaṃ kappetun”ti.
39627.example_1: “Sir, we wish to go to a western land to take up residence there.”

6497.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context “apaloketha, bhikkhave, sāriputtaṃ” the verb is used as a polite request: “take leave of Sāriputta”. The literal sense “look away” is metaphorically extended to “obtain permission”.

36320.meaning_1: many who have gone abroad; those who have gone abroad
36320.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The adjective nānāverajjagata means “many who have gone abroad” or “those who have gone abroad”; it modifies bhikkhu in the accusative, indicating the mendicant being questioned.
36320.example_1: “Reverends, there are those who question a mendicant who has gone abroad—

10491.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the cited passage avighāto describes a state free of distress, anguish and fever, i.e. “untroubled”. It is the negative adjective formed from the verb vighāteti with the negative prefix na.
10491.sutta_1: akusale cāvuso, dhamme upasampajja viharato diṭṭhe ceva dhamme sukho vihāro abhavissa avighāto anupāyāso apariḷāho, kāyassa ca bhedā paraṃ maraṇā sugati pāṭikaṅkhā, nayidaṃ bhagavā akusalānaṃ dhammānaṃ pahānaṃ vaṇṇeyya.
10491.example_1: If those who acquired and kept unskillful qualities were to live happily in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever; and if, when their body breaks up, after death, they could expect to go to a good place, the Buddha would not praise giving up unskillful qualities.

22500.meaning_1: name of a town in Avanti
22500.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Kuraraaghara is a neuter noun meaning “eagle’s house”; it denotes a town in Avanti, literally the house of an eagle. The compound is of the kammadhāraya type (noun + noun).
22500.sutta_1: ekaṃ samayaṃ āyasmā mahākaccāno avantīsu viharati kuraraghare papāte pabbate.
22500.example_1: At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain.

71194.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The noun hāliddikāni is a proper masculine name for a layperson, derived from the feminine “haliddā” (turmeric). In the sutta it appears as the subject “the householder Hāliddikāni”.
71194.sutta_1: atha kho hāliddikāni gahapati yenāyasmā mahākaccāno tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinno kho hāliddikāni gahapati āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ etadavoca:
71194.example_1: Then the householder Hāliddikāni went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him,

71192.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The form hāliddakāni is a plural masculine noun used as a proper name for a householder, derived from haliddā “turmeric”. In the passage it refers to a layperson (gahapati) named after the turmeric term, a common practice for lay names.
71192.example_1: evaṃ me sutaṃ, ekaṃ samayaṃ āyasmā mahākaccāno avantīsu viharati kuraraghare [kulaghare] papāte pabbate. atha kho hāliddikāni [hāliddakāni, haliddikāni] gahapati yenāyasmā mahākaccāno tenupasaṅkami, upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinno kho hāliddikāni gahapati āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ etadavoca, vuttamidaṃ, bhante, bhagavatā aṭṭhakavaggiye māgaṇḍiyapañhe –

71082.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the term refers to a specific layperson named haliddikāni, derived from the root “haliddā” (turmeric). The i‑stem masculine noun takes the plural‑like suffix –āni, which here functions as a proper‑name marker for a householder.
71082.example_1: evaṃ me sutaṃ, ekaṃ samayaṃ āyasmā mahākaccāno avantīsu viharati kuraraghare [kulaghare] papāte pabbate. atha kho hāliddikāni [hāliddakāni, haliddikāni] gahapati yenāyasmā mahākaccāno tenupasaṅkami, upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinno kho hāliddikāni gahapati āyasmantaṃ mahākaccānaṃ etadavoca, vuttamidaṃ, bhante, bhagavatā aṭṭhakavaggiye māgaṇḍiyapañhe –

83867.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of the sentence it describes something that is “not being done” or “unaccomplished”, fitting the negative present participle of “na krubbati”.
83867.example_1:
okaṃ pahāya aniketasārī,
gāme akubbaṃ [akrubbaṃ] muni santhavāni [sandhavāni],
kāmehi ritto apurakkharāno [apurekkharāno (sī, suttanipātepi) moggallāne 5-135 suttampi oloketabbaṃ],
kathaṃ na viggayha janena kayirāti.

53309.meaning_1: name of an eminent grammatical treatise
53309.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the instrumental form “moggallāne” refers to the treatise Moggallāna, indicating that the passage is to be examined according to that grammatical work.
53309.example_1:
okaṃ pahāya aniketasārī,
gāme akubbaṃ [akrubbaṃ] muni santhavāni [sandhavāni],
kāmehi ritto apurakkharāno [apurekkharāno (sī, suttanipātepi) moggallāne 5-135 suttampi oloketabbaṃ],
kathaṃ na viggayha janena kayirāti.

55195.meaning_1: bound by desire to the realm of form
55195.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term describes a being whose consciousness is bound by rāga (desire) toward the rūpadhātu (form element), as seen in the context sentence where it is translated as “bound by desire to the realm of form”.
55195.example_1: One whose consciousness is shackled by greed for the form element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.

70252.meaning_1: bound by desire to the realm of feeling
70252.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound denotes a being whose consciousness is bound by craving (rāga) for the feeling element (vedanā), i.e., a desire‑driven attachment to sensations.
70252.example_1: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the feeling element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.

57413.meaning_1: bound by desire to the realm of perception
57413.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound denotes a being whose consciousness is bound by desire (rāga) to the perception element (saññā dhātu), indicating a mental fetter in the sutta context.
57413.example_1: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the perception element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.

56784.meaning_1: bound by desire to the realm of mental activity
56784.meaning_lit: bound by desire to the mental element
56784.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the cited passage the adjective characterises a being whose consciousness (viññāṇa) is shackled by greed (rāga) to the mental element (saṅkhāradhātu), i.e., a wanderer bound by desire within the mental realm.
56784.example_1: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the choices element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.

16384.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound upāyupādāna means “clinging to a method” and is used in the passage to refer to those whose mind is attached to a method as a source of craving.
16384.example_1: kathañca, gahapati, anokasārī hoti? rūpadhātuyā kho, gahapati, yo chando yo rāgo yā nandī [nandi] yā taṇhā ye upayupādānā [upāyupādānā] cetaso adhiṭṭhānābhinivesānusayā te tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṃkatā [anabhāvakatā, anabhāvaṃgatā] āyatiṃ anuppādadhammā. tasmā tathāgato anokasārīti vuccati.

55200.meaning_1: bound by the expanding home of mental images of matter
55200.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the term describes a person whose mind is bound by the proliferating (visāra) abode of form‑images (rūpa‑nimitta) and dwellings (niketa), i.e., a migrant constantly moving from one mental “abode” to another.
55200.example_1: Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sights, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode.

57985.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the term denotes the “cause of happiness” or a “good sign”, i.e. the factor that leads to a state of bliss. It is a neuter compound of saddha (good, wholesome) and nimitta (sign, cause).
57985.example_1: Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sounds …

24305.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of the sentence, gandhanimitta denotes the sign or mark of a smell – essentially the odor itself.

54706.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.3.6.5 the noun rasanimitta denotes “taste” or “flavor”, literally “that which indicates taste” (rasa = taste, nimitta = sign/indicator).

47829.meaning_1: sign of a splinter; indication of a broken fragment
47829.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.3:6.6 the term denotes the sign or mark indicating a splintered or broken part, which is the object being touched.

34761.meaning_1: bound by the expanding abode of the mental representation of phenomena
34761.meaning_lit: bound by the expanding dwelling of mental representation of phenomena
34761.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context “dhammanimittaniketavisāravinibandhā kho, gahapati, ‘niketasārī’ti vuccati”, the term describes a mind that is bound by the ever‑expanding dwelling (niketa) of its mental representations (nimitta) of phenomena (dhamma), i.e., a “migrant” moving from one mental abode to another.
34761.example_1: thoughts, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode.

33504.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the second sutta in the Hāliddika series of the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN 22.4). The compound is built from the numeral dutiya “second”, the proper name hāliddika, and sutta “discourse”.

56403.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context, the term refers to the collection of questions posed by the deva Sakka, which the Buddha answered; hence the meaning is 'Sakka's questions'.
56403.example_1: “Sir, this was said by the Buddha in ‘The Questions of Sakka’:

59678.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term samādhisutta is the title of a discourse (sutta) in the Saṃyutta Nikāya, meaning “discourse on samādhi”. It is a neuter noun compound of samādhi and sutta.

40861.meaning_1: discourse on the reversal; discourse on the turning back
40861.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta name combines the prefix paṭi‑ (“against, opposite”), the noun sallāṇa (“reversal, turning back”), and sutta (“discourse”), giving the meaning “Discourse on the reversal”. It appears in the Khandha Saṃyutta (SN 22.6).

40860.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.6:1.2 the word occurs in the locative singular paṭisallāṇe, indicating the place of retreat where the bhikkhave are instructed to practice meditation.

54228.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sutta the form appears as yogamāpajjatha (imperative plural), urging monks to take up meditation. The compound literally means ‘goes to yoga (yoke)’, i.e., to engage in meditation.

40874.meaning_1: secluded; retired; withdrawn into seclusion; (comm) hidden in solitude
40874.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence paṭisallīṇo, bhikkhave, bhikkhu yathābhūtaṃ pajānāti, the word is the masculine nominative singular of the past participle, describing a monk who has withdrawn into seclusion and thus truly understands.
40874.example_1: A mendicant in retreat truly understands.

16349.meaning_1: discourse on clinging to the turning of mind
16349.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The title is a kammadhāraya compound meaning a discourse on the relationship between clinging (upādā) and the turning of the mind (paritassana).

16348.meaning_1: agitation through clinging; anxiety through attachment
16348.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sutta the term denotes the mental agitation that arises from clinging (upādā); it is contrasted with the freedom that results when one does not cling.
16348.example_1: “Mendicants, I will teach you how grasping leads to anxiety, and how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety.

4803.meaning_1: non‑agitation through non‑clinging; non‑anxiety through non‑attachment
4803.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.7:6.1 the Buddha asks whether “anupādāaparitassana” (non‑attachment) leads to freedom from anxiety, highlighting the practical outcome of letting go of clinging.
4803.example_1: And how does not grasping lead to freedom from anxiety?

55273.meaning_1: born from the turning of form after change
55273.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the term describes anxieties that arise from the continual turning and change of form, i.e., mental formations that are “born” of the impermanent nature of physical form.
55273.sutta_1: tassa rūpavipariṇāmānuparivattijā paritassanā dhammasamuppādā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.
55273.example_1: Anxieties occupy their mind, born of latching on to the perishing of form, and originating in accordance with natural principles.

70287.meaning_1: rolling around after the change of feeling
70287.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context “tassa vedanāvipariṇāmaññathābhāvā vedanāvipariṇāmānuparivatti viññāṇaṃ hoti”, the adjective describes a feeling that rolls around after it has changed, i.e., a feeling that is produced by the rolling (parivatt) of the feeling‑change (vipariṇāma). The compound is a second‑order (dutiyā) tappurisa type, formed from vedanā + vipariṇāma + anuparivattī.

70286.meaning_1: born from feeling after its change
70286.meaning_lit: born from feeling and the change of feeling
70286.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The adjective describes a feeling that arises after a previous feeling has changed; in the sutta it modifies “citta” indicating a mind conditioned by a sequence of feeling‑changes.
70286.sutta_1: tassa vedanāvipariṇāmānuparivattijā paritassanā dhammasamuppādā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.

56809.meaning_1: rolling around after the change of mental activity
56809.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The adjective describes a mental formation that continues to roll or recur after a change of mental activity, reflecting the compound meaning of saṅkhāra “mental formation”, vipariṇāma “change”, and anuparivattī “following/continuing”.

56808.meaning_1: born from the turning around of mental formations after their change
56808.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence the word appears in the feminine nominative singular (…jā). The base is the compound of saṅkhāra “mental formations”, vipariṇāma “change”, anuparivattin “turning around” and the suffix ja “born of”. Hence the meaning “born from the turning‑around of mental formations after their change”.
56808.sutta_1: tassa saṅkhāravipariṇāmānuparivattijā paritassanā dhammasamuppādā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.

67749.meaning_1: born from rolling around after the change of consciousness
67749.meaning_lit: born from the change of consciousness
67749.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN 22.7:5.4 the term describes anxieties that arise from the continual transformation of consciousness, i.e., “born from the rolling around after the change of consciousness”.
67749.sutta_1: tassa viññāṇavipariṇāmānuparivattijā paritassanā dhammasamuppādā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.
67749.example_1: Anxieties occupy their mind, born of latching on to the perishing of consciousness, and originating in accordance with natural principles.

6396.meaning_1: not obsessing; not overpowering; not occupying; not controlling
6396.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN 22.7:6.7 the term conveys the sense of “not taking all around”, i.e., not being grasped by or dominated by a mental object; it denotes a state free from obsessive or controlling influence.
6396.sutta_1: cetaso apariyādānā na cevuttāsavā hoti na ca vighātavā na ca apekkhavā, anupādāya ca na paritassati.
6396.example_1: So they don’t become frightened, worried, concerned, or anxious because of grasping.

33183.meaning_1: second discourse on the clinging and suffering
33183.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta is the second discourse on upādā (clinging) and paritassana (suffering) in the Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.

21423.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
A sutta title from the Saṃyutta Nikāya (22) referring to a discourse associated with Kālattāya and the principle of impermanence.

21424.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term is the name of a sutta in the Khandha Saṃyutta (SN 22.10). It combines kālā (time) + attaya (threefold) + dukkha (suffering) + sutta (discourse), thus meaning “discourse on the threefold suffering.”

21422.meaning_1: discourse on the three times and non-self
21422.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The title indicates a discourse on the three temporal phases (past, present, future) and the doctrine of non‑self.

71195.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the Hāliddikāni Sutta, a discourse in the Khandha Saṃyutta of the Saṃyutta Nikāya (22). The name is a compound of hāliddika + ni nplural) + sutta, and it is a neuter noun denoting a sutta.

52138.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound denotes the specific question posed by the disciple Māgaṇḍiya in the Sutta Nipāta (4.9, SNP 47). It follows the typical “person + pañha” pattern, with the genitive form of the name (māgaṇḍiya‑assa) combined with pañha “question”.
52138.example_1: “Sir, this was said by the Buddha in the Chapter of the Eights, in ‘The Questions of Māgandiya’:
